chunk.text: The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 CRU International Limited confidential Chapter 2 Cobalt supply Overview The supply of cobalt has become more complex in recent years as an increasing amount of concentrates and crude cobalt intermediates have been produced, traded and used to support larger volumes of refined metal and chemical production, mainly in China and Finland. Our approach in quantifying this supply has therefore been to track the cobalt units produced at the different stages of production and to make allowances for the losses encountered as these raw materials are processed into refined products. We have also adopted a change in methodology for assessing the costs of supplying refined cobalt. The highlights of these analyses are:  Mine production increased to 97,000 tonnes of cobalt in 2011 and came from 51 operations. The largest source of mined cobalt production over this period has been the African Copper Belt with the DRC and Zambia supplying over 60% of this material (59,000 tonnes of cobalt contained).

relations.evidence: The supply of cobalt has become more complex in recent years as an increasing amount of concentrates and crude cobalt intermediates have been produced, traded and used to support larger volumes of refined metal and chemical production

chunk.text: on over this period has been the African Copper Belt with the DRC and Zambia supplying over 60% of this material (59,000 tonnes of cobalt contained). The operations in the African Copper Belt are extremely significant as they are probably going to be the source of choice for increasing supply in the longer term, particularly to China.  Intermediates production in 2011 was just over 35,000 tonnes of cobalt. Overall, this raw material is a part of mine production but generally it represents a crude material that is sold to refiners of metal and chemicals. Much of this material arises from new projects in the DRC and from four recently commissioned HPAL nickel plants. By 2017, we expect this volume to have increased to nearly 70,000 tonnes of contained cobalt.  Refined production grew by 9.5% to just over 78,000 tonnes in 2011. Refined chemicals showed a slightly stronger growth at 11.5% to 45,500 tonnes. At 30,000 tonnes, China was the largest producer but lost production share as a result of slackening market fundamentals. Increased metal production was supported by the return to work in Sudbury and increases in production in China, Zambia and the Moa JV.

relations.evidence: refined production grew by 9.5% to just over 78,000 tonnes in 2011

chunk.text: fundamentals. Increased metal production was supported by the return to work in Sudbury and increases in production in China, Zambia and the Moa JV. Metal production in the DRC declined as a result of an oversupplied market, lower head grades and power constraints.  Production cost estimates have been based on the specific costs of producing cobalt. As a result, the 2011/12 cobalt cost curve is characterised by a large zone (30,000 tonnes) of costs below $3/lb and a similar sized zone of very high costs in the range of $18- 22/lb based on purchased raw materials. In between, there is a small zone of main product producers with costs between $7-14/lb and then a zone of around 19,000 tonnes of production with costs in the range of $4-6.50/lb. The 2017 cost curve is similar in shape with top end prices around $2/lb lower.

relations.evidence: Increased metal production was supported by the return to work in Sudbury and increases in production in China, Zambia and the Moa JV.

chunk.text: 38 The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 2 CRU International Limited confidential 1. Cobalt reserve base Cobalt is usually found in low concentrations and is extracted primarily as a by- or co-product of mining for other metals, mainly nickel and copper. The Cobalt Development Institute estimated that in 2011 55% of cobalt production came from nickel mining, 35% from the mining of copper and other minerals and 10% from primary cobalt sources. Cobalt is widely scattered in the earth‟s crust and a concentration can be found in potentially exploitable quantities in several countries in the world. Significant resources of cobalt are also present in the deep-sea nodules and crusts which occur in the Mid-Pacific, although these are not economically viable with current technology and economic conditions. According to the United States Geological Survey (USGS), as much as 1 billion tons of hypothetical and speculative cobalt resources may exist in manganese nodules and crusts on the ocean floor. Economic concentrations of cobalt can be found in three different geological settings.

relations.evidence: Cobalt is usually found in low concentrations and is extracted primarily as a by- or co-product of mining for other metals, mainly nickel and copper.

chunk.text: may exist in manganese nodules and crusts on the ocean floor. Economic concentrations of cobalt can be found in three different geological settings. The first is in sediment hosted, which can be found in the African Copper Belt, the world‟s most important cobalt source. The second is hydrothermal and volcanogenic, for example at Bou Azzer in Morocco. The third geological setting is magmatic sulphide and related laterite deposits, which can be found in Cuba, New Caledonia, Australia and Russia. 1.1 Conventional assessments Through the years, geologists, mining engineers and other specialists operating in the minerals field have used various terms to describe and classify cobalt mineral reserves and resources. Some of these terms have gained wide use and acceptance, although they are not always used with precisely the same meaning. We use the definition of resources and reserves by the USGS which states that a cobalt resource is a concentration of naturally occurring solid material most often within the Earth‟s crust in such form and amount that economic extraction of a commodity from the concentration can be potentially feasible.

relations.evidence: may exist in manganese nodules and crusts on the ocean floor
economic extraction of a commodity from the concentration can be potentially feasible

chunk.text: st often within the Earth‟s crust in such form and amount that economic extraction of a commodity from the concentration can be potentially feasible. The resource should meet specified minimum physical and chemical criteria in order to be mined viably in regards to grade, quality, thickness and depth. The reserve, however, is considered to be a demonstrated (measured plus indicated) part of the resource which could be economically extracted or produced at the time of determination. Cobalt sources have changed over history, from Norway, Sweden, Hungary and Germany to the dependence on the African Copper Belt. In the last decade, sources have diversified with increasing amounts coming from nickel and copper ores as well as recycled scrap and cobalt containing intermediates. The USGS estimated that the physically available resource and reserve base for cobalt worldwide was 15.2 million tonnes and 7.7 million tonnes respectively in 2011.

chunk.text: The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 2 39 CRU International Limited confidential As Table 2.1 illustrates, the majority of this is located in Africa, Australia and Cuba, while the DRC alone accounts for 44% of global cobalt reserves. Cobalt resources particularly in Africa have scope for more potential. As an example, the Zambian Synclinorium project by Mopani, which in turn is largely owned by Glencore, has measured, indicated and inferred cobalt resources of 320,000 tonnes. Extensive exploration is also taking place in Cameroon by GeoCam. Geovic owns 60.5% of Geovic Cameroon, while the National Investment Corporation of Cameroon, controls 39.5%. Geovic‟s mine permit covers 1,250 square km and provides exclusive production rights to seven large cobalt, nickel and manganese deposits. Two of the seven deposits, Nkamouna and Mada (directly adjacent to Nkamouna), are the first planned for mining and production. Geovic Cameroon has identified the world‟s largest known primary cobalt deposit, with significant nickel and manganese credits.

chunk.text: ining and production. Geovic Cameroon has identified the world‟s largest known primary cobalt deposit, with significant nickel and manganese credits. Independent studies have consistently concluded that the Nkamouna project can be developed with globally competitive CAPEX, OPEX and investment returns. In the Nkamouna and Mada project area, Geovic has delineated a resource of 405,000 tonnes of cobalt and proven and probable reserves totalling 177,000 tonnes. With the copper-cobalt concession at the copperbelt spanning over 15.000 sq km and more than 20 deposits being drilled at present, coupled by 40 years worth of material from just three deposits, the potential for future increase in resources and reserves is significant. At the current rate of primary production, there are around 200 years of supply from stated cobalt resources, indicating an abundance of physically available material. However, we have doubts about the economic viability of some of these resources, particularly in regards to the political instability and the lack of reliable infrastructure (such as roads and power availability) in some African countries.

chunk.text: 40 The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 2 CRU International Limited confidential viable reserves in terms of extraction and processing rather than favourable market, infrastructural and political conditions that make extraction economically viable. We have chosen to restrict our assessments to the proven and probable reserves of the major operators, as defined by JORC1 or equivalent standards. The reserves quoted by companies are defined by their economic viability, reflecting the importance of this on the continuity of their operations. Our analysis of company reports and other sources suggests that the combined reserves of major existing and selected future projects amount to about 2.1 million tonnes, or 30 years of supply at current levels. 1.2 Reserve estimates in China Despite the fact that China is the world‟s largest producer of refined cobalt, in terms of cobalt resources and reserves it remains well behind other leading producers such as the USA and Australia among others. Indeed, in 2011 China‟s resources amounted to almost 500,000 tonnes – only 10% of the DRC‟s resource – with cobalt reserves amounting to 80,000 tonnes.

relations.evidence: Our analysis of company reports and other sources suggests that the combined reserves of major existing and selected future projects amount to about 2.1 million tonnes, or 30 years of supply at current levels.

chunk.text: deed, in 2011 China‟s resources amounted to almost 500,000 tonnes – only 10% of the DRC‟s resource – with cobalt reserves amounting to 80,000 tonnes. The estimates only began to appear from 2007, while prior to that resource estimates were based on primary research in China. The largest deposits are located in the Gansu, Shandong, Hebei and Shanxi provinces and the vast majority of these are believed to be sulphide deposits, with lower cobalt contents than laterite deposits. Our research suggests that laterite deposits have an average nickel to cobalt ratio of 15:1, while sulphide deposits are more in the region of 50:1. China‟s largest nickel producer, Jinchuan, exploits the Gansu mine which is believed to have around 145,000 tonnes of cobalt resources, or a bit less than one third of the country‟s total.

Most of the nickel is found in laterite deposits (58%), which is estimated to have an average grade of 1.59%, whereas the remaining 42% is found in sulphide deposits based on an estimated average nickel grade of 1.02%. Based on the previously outlined nickel/cobalt ratio of 15:1 for laterite deposits and 50:1 for sulphide deposits, we estimate that the total amount of cobalt contained in sulphide and laterite deposits is 1.2 million tonnes and 5.7 million tonnes respectively. chunk.text: The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 2 43 CRU International Limited confidential 2. Mine production After cobalt resources and reserves, the next stage in the production of refined cobalt is mine production. In detail, cobalt production is dependent on ore and concentrates from many sources, some of which are relatively obscure. There are several small nickel mines for example in Australia that produce nickel concentrates that contain cobalt which are shipped overseas for refining in Canada, Finland and China. Similarly, operations in Botswana, Brazil and Canada send mainly nickel concentrates containing cobalt to major smelting and refining operations in their respective countries. We have therefore listed the operations where cobalt is mined in Table 2.3 overleaf. We have identified 51 operations where cobalt ore is mined and show where the respective ores and concentrates are processed. The objective in our tracking of cobalt production at the mine level has been to provide a check on our refined production numbers and particularly to reconcile the volumes of material imported by China and Finland where major volumes of refined metal and chemicals are produced.

relations.evidence: nickel concentrates that contain cobalt
refining in Canada, Finland and China

chunk.text: and particularly to reconcile the volumes of material imported by China and Finland where major volumes of refined metal and chemicals are produced. As identified in Table 2.3, mined production was nearly 73,000 tonnes of contained cobalt in 2009. This increased by nearly 25% year-on-year to just over 90,000 tonnes in 2010 and a further 8% to 97,000 tonnes in 2011. The largest source of mined cobalt production over this period has been the African Copper Belt, with the DRC and Zambia supplying over 60% of this material (59,000 tonnes of cobalt contained). We have identified 14 operations in the DRC, which collectively produced 53,300 tonnes of cobalt in ores and concentrates in 2011. Operations in Russia, Cuba, Australia and Canada contributed a further 19,000 tonnes of contained cobalt during the period while the lateritic deposits in the Pacific found in Indonesia, New Caledonia and the Philippines supplied the bulk of the remainder. The operations in the African Copper Belt are extremely significant as they are probably going to be the source of choice for increasing supply in the longer term, particularly to China.

chunk.text: Belt are extremely significant as they are probably going to be the source of choice for increasing supply in the longer term, particularly to China. The 14 operations currently producing ores and concentrates in the DRC reflect the abundance of deposits and the relative ease in establishing a mine with a heavy media separating plant that can produce concentrates. Some of these operations such as Kov, Kamoto and Tenke Fungurume are large scale integrated mine and metallurgical facilities but nevertheless, it is not inconceivable that future mining operations in the DRC could support production of a further 30-40,000 tonnes of cobalt in the long-term. One other feature that should not be overlooked is that mine production volumes are around 20% higher than refined output. This accounts for the loss in recovery of cobalt units in the refining of both metal and chemicals.

chunk.text: 48 The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 2 CRU International Limited confidential 3. The impact of cobalt raw material flows on refined production A great deal of refined cobalt production has come to rely on concentrates, hydrometallurgical intermediates and alliage blanc, a white alloy of copper, cobalt and iron. Virtually all of the refined cobalt in China, both metal and chemicals, is based on imported concentrates and intermediates. A similar situation with refined chemicals exists in Finland. Since 2000, the production and trade in cobalt concentrates and cobalt intermediates has grown ten-fold. Taking into account that in the processing of these concentrates and intermediates, there is a loss in recovery, we estimate that in 2000, the useable cobalt in concentrates amounted to just over 4,000 tonnes. By 2011, this figure reached over 44,000 tonnes boosted by a growing trade in cobalt intermediates, as indicated in Table 2.4.

In our assessment of the volumes of this production and trade, we have assumed that the useable cobalt in refining operations represents, 75% of cobalt contained in concentrates, 80% of alliage blanc and 85% of hydrometallurgical intermediates such as crude cobalt carbonate, hydroxide and mixed nickel-cobalt sulphides. In 2011, China accounted for nearly 33,000 tonnes of useable cobalt contained in these raw materials and Finland, nearly 12,000 tonnes. Over the past few years, there has been some concern about the apparent disconnect between the volumes of concentrates and intermediates being produced and imported into China.

With this significant expansion in the production and trade of intermediates the concern has been whether a significant surplus of raw materials will be created by 2017 and beyond. Will there be sufficient need and will there be sufficient refining capacity? Overall, we believe that as these hydrometallurgical intermediates are of higher cobalt content than concentrates and they are easier and more efficient to process, these materials will be the first choice of refined metal and chemical producers. In China, we are seeing a rapid increase in the consumption of intermediates and a decline in the use of concentrates (Table 2.4) as well as alliage blanc. Therefore, we are not expecting a surplus of intermediates to be produced, but we will probably see a decline in the use of concentrates out to 2017. In the longer term, many companies that are currently using imported concentrates and intermediates are forecasting expansions to their operations.

chunk.text: The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 2 51 CRU International Limited confidential In Table 2.6, we identify the various refined metal and chemicals producers that use traded concentrates and intermediates and show their forecast production out to 2017 and in 2022. We have also aggregated forecast cobalt containing intermediates, concentrates and lateritic ore availability in useable cobalt units. Out to 2017, we are of the opinion that there will be more than enough potential supply of these materials to meet forecast production. As the production of intermediates increases, we expect the need for concentrates to fall. In fact, our concentrate use forecasts have been calculated as a balancing figure. However in the long-term, that is by 2022, we see that the picture could change quite dramatically. We are showing a small surplus of material on the basis of forecast intermediates supply and a nominal 7,700 tonnes of cobalt in concentrates from the DRC. However this could very easily disappear. Firstly, demand in China for metal and chemicals is expected to be some 25,000 tonnes greater than forecast production by the companies indentified in Table 2.6.

The outlook for new cobalt projects The outlook for new cobalt projects, ones that might contribute to additional production by 2020, has become increasingly uncertain. In 2009, there were 19 potential projects on our list. Several had begun construction and many were in the final stages of financing. Our current supply forecast now includes 12 of these projects listed in 2009, but very few new ones have come forward to a point that they could be regarded serious potential supply sources.

The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 2 55 CRU International Limited confidential 5. Cobalt refined production in 2011 In spite of well anticipated and often substantial production cuts at leading refined producers around the world last year, such as Katanga Mining in the DRC, primary cobalt production rose again emphatically overall to 78,296 tonnes, gaining 9.5%. In 2011, output of cobalt specialty products and chemicals rose most strongly; by 11.8% to 45,476 tonnes. This segment of the market accounted for a 58% share of total primary output, up from 57% in 2010. Interestingly, China‟s production share of specialty products fell last year, largely because of a large decline at leading operations Zhejiang Huayou and Jinchuan, both of whom who had been unwilling, given slackening market fundamentals, to maintain full capacity as in 2010. Indeed Huayou, a 6,000 tpy chemicals producer by capacity, produced 4,000 tonnes last year, down from 5,800 tonnes in 2010; a level supported by an excess of raw material the company had been keen to draw down.

relations.evidence: Cobalt refined production in 2011

chunk.text: oduced 4,000 tonnes last year, down from 5,800 tonnes in 2010; a level supported by an excess of raw material the company had been keen to draw down. Overall, however, specialty production in China still rose impressively to 29,839 tonnes last year or 66% of the global total, supported by Huayou‟s neighbour Galico, Ganzhou Hengsheng Umicore and some smaller facilities. The largest growth, however, came from outside of China, for example India, albeit from a low starting point, but also the Yabulu refinery in Australia, which had another strong growth year in reaching 2,631 tonnes up from 2,189 tonnes in 2010. On the metal producing side, Katanga Mining – the third largest metal producer in 2010 with the reported potential to become number one – was not alone in recording a decline, though the 29.2% retreat to just 2,433 tonnes was the largest of all major producers. While the company continued to be impacted by an oversupplied market, lower cobalt head grades and electricity constraints were major factors in last year‟s decline. Electricity constraints affected various operations last year, particularly in the final quarter.

In Norway, meanwhile, Xstrata recorded a 4.4% decline of their high grade cobalt refined at Nikkelverk – a 5,200 tpy processor – though they remained fourth largest producer globally by exceeding 3,000 tonnes. Nevertheless last year was another characterised by solid advances in the metal producing sector overall. The 6.4% gain to 32,820 tonnes was supported in particular by Vale‟s so-called “return to normalcy” in Sudbury, Canada following a lengthy strike. This took production from 1,065 tonnes to 2,675 tonnes (including 1,585 tonnes refined in Voisey Bay). This ensured Vale became the fifth largest producer overall. Jinchuan, meanwhile, the fifth largest metal producer in 2010, moved up the leader board to third, with 3,143 tonnes and the largest producers Chambishi Metals and ICCI (50% owned by Sherritt through the Moa Joint Venture) surged to an estimated 4,856 tonnes and 3,854 tonnes respectively.

58 The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 2 CRU International Limited confidential 6. Cobalt refined production forecast Although further erosion in cobalt supply can be expected from various producers this year again, including Katanga Mining, who continued to be affected by power disruptions in the DRC during the first quarter, we expect another year of strong albeit slowing growth in refined production. Unlike the past few years, however, when specialty, chemical grade supply growth has driven the refined market, we still believe metal will become the driver, albeit partly to fill a gap in specialty supply caused by the downstream production plans underway in the DRC. In fact this year we forecast a decline in chemical cobalt production of 1.0%, before a relatively modest 3.8% per year rise is forecast out to 2017. CRU predicts that small Chinese chemical producers will cut production in 2012, affected by low market prices. Like last year, however, there will be a mix of developments from one producer to another, particularly within China.

Clearly, profitability has been affected over the past year by a weakening cobalt price – their adjusted operating profit slipped to US$81m in the main cobalt division, Advanced Materials in 2011 – but their outlook remains robust, supported particularly by demand for tablets and smartphones. During the next five years we forecast the company‟s growth will exceed the rest of the specialty sector. The CAGR from 2012 to 2017 is predicted to be 6.0%, compared to 3.1% for other specialty producers. Though impressive, OMG‟s potential pales next to metal producers who as a combined group will surpass the specialty industry by 2015 and represent 52% of the refined market by 2017. Leading the way will not be Chambishi Metals, ICCI (Moa Joint Venture), or Jinchuan, who together accounted for 36% of global metal production last year.

In fact by 2017, these three facilities are forecast to account for just 11% of the market, thanks in large part to ENRC‟s planned conversion of Chambishi into a copper producer with less than 1,000 tpy of cobalt capacity (20% of current production). In addition to the growth in primary production, CRU believes that over the forecast period secondary production will gather momentum. We predict that environmental and cost pressures will lead to a rise in the recycling of batteries. As cobalt is a valuable metal, an increase in recycling volumes will minimise losses. According to Umicore, cobalt use in the battery end- use has increased by seven times in the last 13 years. CRU projects that with the aggressive sales growth of electronic devices and the strong potential for EV/HEV, battery recycling represents an alternative source of cobalt. This year, we introduced a new series under secondary production, named „Recycling,‟ which captures Umicore‟s battery smelting.

chunk.text: The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 2 61 CRU International Limited confidential 7. Costs of production In our outlook, we have adopted a different approach to estimating the costs of producing cobalt. Instead of using our value-based costing method, which allocated cobalt production costs in accordance with the value of cobalt and the main metal, we have attempted to estimate the specific costs of cobalt production at the key operations. We have made this change because it is essential to understand how the cobalt supply chain is going to respond to the rise in processing of cobalt concentrates and intermediates in China and the rapid increase in the amounts of cobalt intermediates, such as crude cobalt hydroxide or carbonate that are being produced at current copper and nickel operations and projects. The supply chain has become elongated and in detail, more complex. Table 2.10 below shows our grouping of the different types of cobalt production and how the volumes of production are expected to evolve by 2017.

chunk.text: ex. Table 2.10 below shows our grouping of the different types of cobalt production and how the volumes of production are expected to evolve by 2017. Currently, the Chinese operations, which in the main produce refined cobalt chemicals from purchased concentrates and intermediates, have the highest cost base and as such, they represent the “marginal cost” section of the supply cost curve. Some of the recent transaction prices charged for concentrates and intermediates may not be sustainable in the long term. By contrast, around 60% of all refined cobalt, mainly metal, is produced as a by-product in the late stages of copper and nickel refining at nickel refineries and integrated metal operations. The specific cost of production of cobalt in these operations, particularly at the nickel refineries, is therefore very much lower as the main metal assumes most of the cost of mining and processing. In addition to these three groups of producers are the main product cobalt producers and, as mentioned earlier, the intermediates producers that produce crude cobalt hydroxide, sulphide and carbonate in hydrometallurgical operations and sell this to third parties.

The economics of cobalt supply have therefore become more dependent on the transfer pricing of concentrates and intermediates and their refining costs. We therefore believe that the most appropriate approach to establishing the current supply curve is to estimate the specific costs of producing cobalt at the key operations and delivering it to the market, as it is these costs that will determine the future behaviour of producers of refined cobalt. We stress at this point that our figures must be regarded as estimates as we have at times been forced to use some typical industry operating parameters at some operations. However, in defence of our approach, we believe we have been reasonably consistent in our assessments and by modelling the costs of each operation within these five different groups and this has enabled us to identify some of the critical cost points for each group.

chunk.text: g the costs of each operation within these five different groups and this has enabled us to identify some of the critical cost points for each group. This section of the report therefore begins with a short description of our methodology and source materials. We then describe the key features of the 2011/12 cost curve and explain how we expect this will evolve by 2017. 7.1 Estimates of specific cobalt production costs Our estimates of cobalt production costs for by-product cobalt have come from the CRU in- house copper and nickel production cost models. Virtually all of by-product cobalt is produced in the nickel refineries and the integrated metal producers and collectively, this represents the largest volume of refined cobalt production. We have focused exclusively on the specific costs of producing cobalt at these operations. Generally speaking this has involved assessing the costs in the purification of the cobalt leach liquor and the electro-winning of cobalt metal or the case of the Sherritt process, the hydrometallurgical precipitation, sintering and briquetting of the metal.

chunk.text: he electro-winning of cobalt metal or the case of the Sherritt process, the hydrometallurgical precipitation, sintering and briquetting of the metal. Our cost estimates have been prepared from a bottom-up approach, assessing the costs of power, labour and consumables for the purification and electro-winning or briquetting stages. We have also estimated the costs of delivering the cobalt produced into the key markets of the Western Hemisphere and China where appropriate. Where we have relevant information, we have included the costs of maintenance, sustaining and working capital. The operations where we have included these costs are generally the integrated metal producers where we have assembled detailed cost profiles in the CRU copper and nickel cost models. In the nickel refineries, where cobalt is produced at a very late stage in the refining process, we have assumed that these costs would be absorbed by the main metal, nickel. For operations where cobalt is the main product, we have taken typical, mining, milling, smelting and refining costs for the operations involved.

chunk.text: The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 2 63 CRU International Limited confidential pyrometallurgical operations that process cobalt-rich slag, so relevant operating parameters for each operation had to be selected. The CRU nickel cost model also contains profiles of some main product cobalt operations. In our assessment of the Chinese processing operations, we have looked at the three types of cobalt raw materials imported into China at present: alliage blanc, cobalt concentrates and hydro metallurgical intermediates. We have used our estimated cobalt contents of these materials, recovery rates and the current pricing practices for these materials. For example, we have assumed that in the case of cobalt concentrates, they contain 6% cobalt, recovery is 75% and payment is made at 60% of the metal price. For hydrometallurgical intermediates such as crude carbonate and hydroxide, cobalt content is much higher at 25%, recovery is also better at 85% and price paid is 70% of the metal price.

We have also estimated overland freight costs out of the DRC, ocean freight and internal Chinese freight charges in order to assess the full costs of these materials before they are leached and converted to chemicals. In assessing the costs of delivering refined cobalt to market, we have continued to use the “realisation cost” estimates from our cost models. Realisation costs include freight charges from the production centre into a major market, marketing and sales costs and interest on the cost of the pipeline that is required to be established for delivery. Where a crude intermediate is produced, our approach assumes that either a refining cost or a refining charge will need to be estimated in order to fully quantify the realisations costs. In the case of the OMG Big Hill operation, alliage blanc is sent to the company‟s operations in Finland for further processing. In this case we assume the material attracts a refining cost as part of the realisations costs.

chunk.text: mpany‟s operations in Finland for further processing. In this case we assume the material attracts a refining cost as part of the realisations costs. Similarly, the crude carbonate produced at the Ruashi operation in the DRC (recently purchased by Jinchuan) incurs a refining cost as well, as this material will be processed at the Jinchuan plant in China. However, in the case of the crude hydroxide produced by Tenke Fungurume, we assume that this incurs a refining charge as this is effectively sold to a third party. In estimating the refining charge we have used current tolling tariffs. On this question of refining charges and costs, we have assumed that the specific costs of producing cobalt from mixed nickel cobalt intermediates is zero until the last stage of refining. This means that the specific costs of producing cobalt from this material will resemble the cobalt production costs of the nickel refineries. 7.2 Cobalt costs 2011/12 Our detailed cost estimates of cobalt production costs in 2011/12 are provided in Table 2.11 towards the end of this section. chunk.text: n the average MB price of cobalt was around $16/lb. Interestingly, both the high and low cost figures were based on production at Chinese operations. The high cost figure represents the cost of processing purchased materials and supplying a local Chinese market. We have seen that the lowest cost operations using purchased materials are the ones processing hydrometallurgical intermediates. Concentrate processing operations are the most expensive. One other feature of note is that the cost of processing refined cobalt is higher than metal price quotations such as the MB or LME by about $4/lb and this represented 25,000 tonnes of refined cobalt production in 2011. The lowest cost estimate of producing refined cobalt is at the Jinchuan operations. This is a result of the fact that cobalt refining takes place at the late stage of nickel and copper refining and that the main metals bears virtually all the cost until the cobalt feed is purified and electro- winning takes place. Jinchuan has lower labour and consumables costs than the other nickel refineries which give it a small cost advantage.

chunk.text: electro- winning takes place. Jinchuan has lower labour and consumables costs than the other nickel refineries which give it a small cost advantage. The key part of this analysis is that the combined production of both the nickel refineries and the integrated metal producers have an effective cost of production and sales of cobalt of below $3/lb. These two groups of producers represented 31,000 tonnes of production in 2011. In the middle ground, are the three main product cobalt producers: Kasese, Chambishi Slag Processing (Cosac) and CTT which have costs of $7.40/lb; $14.38/lb and $9.11/lb respectively. The Chambishi slag operation suffers from having to process a much lower grade of slag than the OMG Big Hill operation and a very much higher power tariff, which for a pyrometallurgical operation is most significant. Currently, this operation can only be marginally profitable. The other important feature is the costs of producing intermediates. The Ruashi operation is currently producing cobalt hydroxide which is being shipped for processing at Jinchuan‟s refinery. As such it has very low site costs but incurs a refining cost as part of the operation‟s realisation costs.

chunk.text: ed for processing at Jinchuan‟s refinery. As such it has very low site costs but incurs a refining cost as part of the operation‟s realisation costs. Effective production and sales costs are $3.56/lb but the site costs, which represent the cost of producing the carbonate are only $0.39/lb. From a mining and processing viewpoint, these costs are low and from Jinchuan‟s perspective, this is a very low cost operation in terms of producing refined cobalt in China. It does appear that the production of hydrometallurgical intermediates for refining in China is currently very profitable and currently represents around 19,000 tonnes of refined cobalt. The Tenke Fungurume cost estimate which includes a tolling charge, has total costs of around $6.42/lb. The full cost in China however when using purchased material is almost three times that figure. There are therefore obvious advantages for Chinese processors and other companies with processing capability in taking equity stakes or even full ownership in DRC operations producing intermediates. The OMG Big Hill operation is also testimony to this fact, even though the pyrometallurgical route is more expensive.

chunk.text: 66 The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 2 CRU International Limited confidential The 2011/12 cobalt cost curve is therefore characterised by a large zone of costs below $3/lb and another slightly smaller zone of very high costs of around $18-22/lb. In between, there is a small zone of main product producers with costs between $7-14/lb and then a zone of around 19,000 tonnes of production with costs in the range of $4-6.50/lb. There is no doubt that the current marginal section of the cost curve is predicated on purchased raw materials and one which favours the hydrometallurgical intermediates. We have also seen that when a Chinese or other company such as OMG, takes an equity stake in an intermediate operation, their production costs fall significantly. We therefore expect to see some significant changes in the shape of the cost curve over the next five years. 7.3 Forecast cobalt costs 2017 Our detailed cost estimates of nominal cobalt production costs in 2017 are provided in Table 2.12 towards the end of this section.

chunk.text: cobalt costs 2017 Our detailed cost estimates of nominal cobalt production costs in 2017 are provided in Table 2.12 towards the end of this section. As can be seen the 2017 cost curve overleaf, we estimate that effective production and sales costs of refined cobalt ranged from a low of $1.90/lb to a high of just under $21/lb with a forecast average price of cobalt of $14/lb in 2017. The key characteristics of this cost curve are very similar to the 2011/12 cost curve but the volume of production will be considerably higher as shown in Table 2.9. Refined production covered in this analysis is close to 100,000 tonnes. In addition to this we are expecting nearly 32,000 tonnes of cobalt contained in intermediates to be produced and a further 11,000 tonnes of cobalt contained in mixed nickel cobalt intermediates. We are expecting around 55,000 tonnes of cobalt, which is over half of all refined cobalt production, to be produced at nickel refineries and integrated metal producers which will have specific production and sales costs of between $2 -3.12/lb.

relations.evidence: Refined production covered in this analysis is close to 100,000 tonnes.

chunk.text: ction, to be produced at nickel refineries and integrated metal producers which will have specific production and sales costs of between $2 -3.12/lb. We estimate that there will be 32,000 tonnes of mainly hydrometallurgical intermediates that after refining will have production and marketing costs of between $5-7.72/lb. The main product producers are expected to change by 2017. We expect Kasese to have depleted its reserves but we expect the Idaho project to be operational by this time. We estimate that effective production and sales costs in this group will be in the range of $9-15/lb. It is quite possible that at a cobalt price of $14/lb, the Chambishi Slag operation may be forced into care and maintenance. In China, we expect to see a further increase in production to around 35,000 tonnes of cobalt per annum from purchased raw materials. We are also expecting this to be sourced mainly from hydrometallurgical intermediates. Effective production and marketing costs will be expected to range from $16.41 to $20.76/lb.

chunk.text: sentially this treatment of cobalt costs shows that around half of forecast production in 2017 (55,000 tonnes) can be produced around $3/lb or below. Another section of production, around 32,000 tonnes producing intermediates, has costs ranging between $5-8/lb. At operations where cobalt is produced as a main product, 8,000 tonnes in total, costs range between $9-15/lb. The marginal section of the cost curve is set by production of mainly refined chemicals in China. Working on the assumption that cobalt raw materials are purchased, we estimate that cobalt production of around 35,000 tonnes has effective production and sales costs of $16.41 - 20.76/lb. Our main observation here is that the marginal cost of production is being kept artificially high by the transaction prices of raw materials, mainly from the DRC. If we look at the production of the hydrometallurgical intermediates which are the preferred material in China for reasons of transport and processing, the cost of production is $5-8/lb. This includes a refining cost in our realisations costs, which is the cost of delivering a refined product to market.

There are therefore very good reasons for Chinese companies attempting to reduce their current costs of purchase by taking stakes in operations producing these hydrometallurgical intermediates. Not all of these are in the DRC, projects in The Philippines, Papua New Guinea and Australia are currently being commissioned and these could change the cost profile of refined chemical production in China. In fact we note that Jinchuan and the Sumitomo refinery at Niihama are currently using these types of feed and are currently among the lowest cost cobalt producers.
